tavily.raw: {"query":"H5N1 human cases deaths global count December 2025 WHO update","follow_up_questions":null,"answer":null,"images":[],"results":[{"url":"https://www.paho.org/sites/default/files/2025-03/2025-mar-4-phe-epidupdate-avianinfluenza-eng-final.pdf","title":"Epidemiological Update Avian Influenza A(H5N1) in the ...","content":"Mar 4, 2025 — 964 human cases of avian influenza A(H5N1), including 466 deaths (48% case fatality), were reported to the World Health Organization (WHO) from","score":0.7336813,"raw_content":null},{"url":"https://www.cdc.gov/bird-flu/spotlights/global-summary-06262026.html","title":"Global Summary of Human Cases of H5N1 Bird Flu from August 2025 to June 2026","content":"A **.gov** website belongs to an official government organization in the United States. # Global Summary of Human Cases of H5N1 Bird Flu from August 2025 to June 2026. *   From August 4, 2025 to June 10, 2026, 12 human infections with avian influenza A(H5N1) viruses (H5N1 bird flu) were reported by three countries outside of the United States. *   The 12 cases were reported by Bangladesh, Cambodia, and India. This report focuses on human cases of H5N1 bird flu reported internationally since August 4, 2025, when CDC last published a summary of human infections with avian influenza A(H5N1) viruses (H5N1 bird flu) reported internationally. These A(H5N1) virus infections occurred in three countries: Bangladesh, Cambodia, and India. Most A(H5N1) virus infections in other countries have been associated with a different clade (i.e., genetic grouping) of A(H5N1) viruses than cases reported in the United States, and generally have a high mortality rate. Three human bird flu cases with A(H5N1) virus infection (clade 2.3.2.1a) were reported to have occurred in Bangladesh since August 2025.","score":0.7135319,"raw_content":null},{"url":"https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2020%E2%80%932026_H5N1_outbreak","title":"2020–2026 H5N1 outbreak","content":"The virus strain involved in the global outbreak is as H5N1 clade 2.3.4.4b which has adapted to cause significant outbreaks in a broader range of species, mammals included. H5N6 and H5N8 viruses with the H5-2.3.4.4b hemagglutinin \"Hemagglutinin (influenza)\") (HA) gene became prominent globally in 2018–2020 (see 2020–2023 H5N8 outbreak) In 2020, reassortment (genetic \"swapping\") between these H5-2.3.4.4b viruses and other strains \"Strain (biology)\") of avian influenza led to the emergence of a H5N1 strain with a H5-2.3.4.4b gene. As of June 2026, H5N1 avian influenza is. A human case of H5N1 was reported in the U.S. in April, \"though this detection may have been the result of contamination of the nasal passages with the virus rather than actual infection.\" In September, Spain reported a human case; this was followed by a second case in November, in a person who worked at the same poultry farm as the first.","score":0.55912215,"raw_content":null},{"url":"https://www.fao.org/animal-health/situation-updates/global-aiv-with-zoonotic-potential/en","title":"Global AIV with Zoonotic Potential","content":"## Animal health. ### **Global Avian Influenza Viruses with Zoonotic Potential situation update**. This update covers avian influenza viruses (AIV) with zoonotic potential occurring worldwide, i.e. H5Nx, H7Nx high pathogenicity avian influenza (HPAI) viruses and H3N8, H5Nx, H6N1, H7Nx, H9N2, H10Nx and H11 low pathogenicity avian influenza (LPAI). Specific information is available for Avian Influenza A(H7N9) virus-virus/en) viruses and Sub-Saharan Africa HPAI  in related FAO Avian Influenza situation updates. **HPAI outbreaks in animals** officially reported since last update (23 April 2026): in total, 763 outbreaks / events have been reported in 31 countries/territories caused by H5Nx (95), H5N1 (659), H5N5 (3), H5N9 (3) and H7N3 (3) (see Table 1 for details). **Number of human cases** officially reported since the last update: 5 new events were reported. **Map 2.** *Global distribution of AIV with zoonotic potential\\* observed in the period 1 October 2024 to 30 September 2025 (i.e. previous wave)*. **Table 1.**High pathogenicity avian influenza viruses with zoonotic potential reported **since the last update**.","score":0.4042963,"raw_content":null},{"url":"https://www.who.int/emergencies/disease-outbreak-news/item/2025-DON590","title":"Avian Influenza A(H5N5)- United States of America","content":"# Avian Influenza A(H5N5) - United States of America. On 15 November 2025, WHO was notified of the 71st confirmed human case with influenza A(H5) since early 2024 in the United States of America— the first human case reported in the United States of America since February 2025. On 20 November, U.S. Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC) laboratory sequencing verified the virus as influenza A(H5N5), representing the first globally reported human case caused by an influenza A(H5N5) virus. On 15 November 2025, WHO was notified of a confirmed human infection with influenza A(H5) in the United States of America—the first reported in the country since February 2025 and the 71st since early 2024. High pathogenicity avian influenza A(H5) clade 2.3.4.4b A(H5N5) viruses have been detected in North America in wild birds and wild mammals since at least 2023.[2] This is the first laboratory-confirmed human infection with an influenza A(H5N5) virus in the United States of America and reported globally.","score":0.37360206,"raw_content":null}],"response_time":1.53,"request_id":"cd57d869-6bac-4db3-99c2-f3ac19ca064c"}
